tongue.sig: Cold, dampness, early infection or digestive weakness. Kapha excess and Ama (undigested toxins) accumulation.
tongue.cond: Upper respiratory tract infection, acute gastritis, hypothyroidism, early stages of febrile illness
tongue.inv: TSH, CBC, Helicobacter pylori antigen, fasting glucose

tongue.sig: Heat, inflammation, hepatobiliary stress, or gastrointestinal infection. Pitta excess and gut dysbiosis.
tongue.cond: Gastritis, GERD, hepatitis, appendicitis, cholecystitis, jaundice
tongue.inv: LFT (bilirubin, ALT, AST), H. pylori test, serum amylase, lipase

tongue.sig: Severe dysbiosis, extreme dehydration, prolonged antibiotic use, or very high toxic burden. Critically impaired digestive ecology.
tongue.cond: Bismuth ingestion, black hairy tongue (fungal overgrowth), severe systemic infection, extreme dehydration
tongue.inv: Stool culture, CBC, urea and electrolytes, hydration markers, candida antibody titre

tongue.sig: Ama accumulation — excess undigested matter in the gut. Significantly impaired gastric fire (Agni). Strong marker of gut permeability issues.
tongue.cond: IBS, SIBO, candidiasis, malabsorption syndromes, leaky gut syndrome
tongue.inv: Lactulose-mannitol permeability test, stool culture, SIBO breath test, food intolerance panel

tongue.sig: Blood or Qi (vital force) deficiency. Anaemia, poor peripheral circulation, or chronic nutrient depletion.
tongue.cond: Iron deficiency anaemia, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, hypothyroidism, chronic fatigue syndrome
tongue.inv: CBC with differential, serum ferritin, serum B12, folate, TSH

tongue.sig: Excess heat, systemic inflammation, or Pitta aggravation. Red tip = cardiac or emotional stress. Red sides = hepatic heat.
tongue.cond: Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency (glossitis), hyperthyroidism, febrile illness, autoimmune states
tongue.inv: Serum B12, folate, TFT (TSH, FT3, FT4), inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR)

tongue.sig: Blood stasis, poor venous return, or hypoxia. Strong indicator of circulatory dysfunction — central or peripheral cyanosis.
tongue.cond: Deep vein thrombosis, peripheral vascular disease, heart failure, severe respiratory insufficiency, Raynaud's phenomenon
tongue.inv: SpO2 monitoring, ECG, D-dimer, echocardiography, Doppler ultrasound lower limbs

tongue.sig: Immune dysregulation, stress reactivity, Pitta-Liver imbalance. Correlates with gut-immune axis disruption.
tongue.cond: Psoriasis (strong association), IBS, atopic dermatitis, coeliac disease, B-vitamin deficiency
tongue.inv: Food intolerance panel, anti-tTG IgA, diurnal cortisol, B-complex vitamins

tongue.sig: Dampness and Kapha — fluid retention, lymphatic stagnation, or hypothyroid state. Often shows scalloping from tooth impressions.
tongue.cond: Hypothyroidism, congestive heart failure, hypoproteinaemia, angioedema, nutritional deficiency
tongue.inv: TSH, FT4, serum albumin, BNP, CBC, urine protein

tongue.sig: Yin deficiency — chronic wasting, malnutrition, or long-standing dehydration. Depleted constitutional reserves.
tongue.cond: Severe dehydration, anorexia nervosa, cachexia, chronic debilitating illness
tongue.inv: Serum albumin, pre-albumin, electrolytes, serum B12, anthropometric measures

tongue.sig: Yin deficiency or heat consuming body fluids. May indicate riboflavin or niacin deficiency. Chronic fissuring suggests long-standing malabsorption.
tongue.cond: Sjogren's syndrome, dehydration, vitamin B2 or B3 deficiency, normal ageing variant
tongue.inv: Schirmer's test, ANA, anti-Ro/La, riboflavin, niacin metabolites

tongue.sig: Spleen Qi deficiency — impaired nutrient absorption and fluid metabolism. Classic naturopathic sign of poor digestive fire.
tongue.cond: Malabsorption syndrome, hypothyroidism, fluid retention, chronic fatigue
tongue.inv: TSH, serum albumin, stool fat (faecal elastase), magnesium, zinc

tongue.sig: Yin deficiency or fluid depletion. May indicate autonomic dysfunction, salivary gland hypofunction, or anticholinergic medication effect.
tongue.cond: Sjogren's syndrome, diabetes mellitus, anticholinergic drug effects, dehydration, mouth breathing
tongue.inv: Fasting blood glucose, HbA1c, ANA, anti-Ro/La antibodies, salivary flow rate

tongue.sig: Yang deficiency — metabolic slowing, Kapha excess. Internal cold with inability to transform fluids.
tongue.cond: Hypothyroidism, renal failure, neurological conditions (Parkinson's, stroke), heavy metal toxicity
tongue.inv: TSH, eGFR, heavy metals panel, neurological assessment

tongue.sig: Internal Wind or nervous system dysregulation — Vata imbalance. Indicates neurological or metabolic instability.
tongue.cond: Parkinson's disease, essential tremor, anxiety, hyperthyroidism, alcohol withdrawal
tongue.inv: TSH (FT3, FT4), neurological examination, GGT, MCV, electrolytes

tongue.sig: Asymmetric hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) dysfunction — deviates toward the side of the lesion. Critical neurological red flag.
tongue.cond: Stroke (CVA), space-occupying CNS lesion, hypoglossal nerve palsy
tongue.inv: URGENT — CT/MRI head, cranial nerve examination, BP, ECG, carotid Doppler

nail.sig: Chronic tissue hypoxia or chronic inflammatory state. Loss of nail-bed angle (Lovibond angle >180 degrees). Drumstick appearance of terminal phalanges.
nail.cond: Lung carcinoma, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis, infective endocarditis, Crohn's disease, cirrhosis, congenital cyanotic heart disease
nail.inv: Chest X-ray, pulmonary function tests, CT chest, LFT, echocardiography, blood cultures

nail.sig: Softening and spooning — classically indicates iron deficiency and poor mucosal integrity. Also seen in haemochromatosis (excess iron).
nail.cond: Iron deficiency anaemia, haemochromatosis, Plummer-Vinson syndrome, hypothyroidism
nail.inv: Serum ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, CBC, TSH

nail.sig: Transverse grooves — temporary arrest of nail matrix growth. Timing can be estimated: nails grow approximately 1 mm per month, so line position dates the insult.
nail.cond: Acute severe illness (myocardial infarction, pneumonia), chemotherapy, zinc deficiency, malnutrition, high fever
nail.inv: Zinc, albumin, CBC, review illness timeline (lines appear 4 to 8 weeks after insult)

nail.sig: Hypoalbuminaemia (Terry's variant) or zinc deficiency. True leuconychia involves the nail plate; apparent leuconychia involves the nail bed.
nail.cond: Hypoalbuminaemia (liver disease, nephrotic syndrome), zinc deficiency, arsenic toxicity, trauma
nail.inv: Serum albumin, LFT, 24h urine protein, serum zinc, heavy metals panel

nail.sig: Proximal two-thirds white, distal 1 to 2 mm pink or brown band. Strong indicator of systemic hypoalbuminaemia. High specificity for cirrhosis.
nail.cond: Hepatic cirrhosis, congestive heart failure, type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic renal failure
nail.inv: LFT, albumin, BNP, HbA1c, eGFR, echocardiography

nail.sig: Proximal half white or opaque, distal half brown-pink. Pathognomonic for chronic kidney disease. Uraemic toxins alter nail bed melanin distribution.
nail.cond: Chronic kidney disease (most specific), uraemia, Crohn's disease (rare)
nail.inv: eGFR, serum creatinine, BUN, urine analysis (protein, casts), electrolytes

nail.sig: Small punctate depressions from focal parakeratosis of the nail matrix. Hallmark of inflammatory skin conditions, especially psoriasis.
nail.cond: Psoriasis (most common), psoriatic arthritis, alopecia areata, eczema, Reiter's syndrome
nail.inv: Skin assessment, rheumatoid factor, HLA-B27, ANA, dermatology referral

nail.sig: Separation of nail plate from nail bed. May be primary (thyroid, trauma) or secondary (infection, psoriasis). Yellow-green discolouration suggests fungal infection.
nail.cond: Hyperthyroidism (classic association), psoriasis, tinea unguium (onychomycosis), trauma, drug reactions
nail.inv: TFT (TSH, FT3, FT4), KOH preparation, fungal culture, skin patch test if drug-related

nail.sig: Linear dark streaks — proximal lesions suggest systemic cause (vasculitis, endocarditis); distal lesions often trauma. Microemboli in nail-bed capillaries.
nail.cond: Infective endocarditis (critical concern), systemic vasculitis, antiphospholipid syndrome, scurvy, trauma (common cause)
nail.inv: Blood cultures (x3), echocardiography, ANCA, antiphospholipid antibodies, vitamin C levels, CRP

nail.sig: Transverse white bands running across full nail width — distinct from Beau's lines (which are grooves). Classic sign of heavy metal poisoning or uraemic stress.
nail.cond: Arsenic poisoning (most classic), thallium toxicity, chemotherapy, renal failure, carbon monoxide poisoning
nail.inv: 24h urine arsenic, heavy metals panel (arsenic, thallium, lead), eGFR, BUN, carboxyhaemoglobin

eye.sig: Lipid deposition in peripheral corneal stroma. Normal in patients over 60 years. In patients under 45, strongly indicates dyslipidaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk.
eye.cond: Familial hypercholesterolaemia (in young patients), mixed dyslipidaemia, normal ageing variant (over 60 years)
eye.inv: Fasting lipid profile (LDL, HDL, triglycerides, total cholesterol), cardiovascular risk stratification (Framingham score), LFT

eye.sig: Yellowish lipid-laden plaques on eyelids. Half of patients have normal lipids — signals hepatobiliary stress and altered lipid metabolism even with normal serum values.
eye.cond: Familial hypercholesterolaemia, primary biliary cholangitis, hypothyroidism, diabetes mellitus, nephrotic syndrome
eye.inv: Fasting lipid profile, LFT (including GGT, alkaline phosphatase), TSH, fasting glucose, urine albumin

eye.sig: Hyperbilirubinaemia (>34 micromol per litre or >2 mg per dL). Bilirubin deposits in scleral elastin. Scleral icterus appears before skin jaundice. Sclera is yellow in jaundice but spared in carotenemia.
eye.cond: Hepatitis (viral, autoimmune, toxic), biliary obstruction (choledocholithiasis, cholangiocarcinoma), haemolytic anaemia, Gilbert's syndrome
eye.inv: Serum bilirubin (total, direct, indirect), LFT (ALT, AST, GGT, ALP), CBC, reticulocyte count, USS abdomen

eye.sig: Greenish-brown ring at peripheral cornea (Descemet's membrane) — copper deposition. Pathognomonic for Wilson's disease. Requires slit-lamp examination.
eye.cond: Wilson's disease (hepatolenticular degeneration), primary biliary cirrhosis (rare)
eye.inv: Serum ceruloplasmin (decreased in Wilson's), 24h urine copper (elevated), liver copper biopsy, genetic testing (ATP7B mutation), slit-lamp ophthalmology

eye.sig: Pallor of lower palpebral conjunctiva reflects reduced haemoglobin. Pull down lower eyelid — pale pink or white indicates haemoglobin typically below 9 g per dL.
eye.cond: Iron deficiency anaemia, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, thalassaemia, chronic disease anaemia, haemolytic anaemia
eye.inv: CBC with differential, serum ferritin, serum B12, folate, peripheral blood smear, reticulocyte count

eye.sig: Forward protrusion of the eyeball. Most commonly thyroid-associated (Graves' ophthalmopathy). Pitta excess and thyroid fire in naturopathic assessment.
eye.cond: Graves' disease (most common cause), hyperthyroidism, orbital cellulitis, retro-orbital tumour, cavernous sinus thrombosis
eye.inv: TSH, FT3, FT4, TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb), thyroid USS, CT orbit, ophthalmology referral

eye.sig: Foamy, triangular, greyish-white patches on bulbar conjunctiva (usually temporal). Keratin and bacteria accumulation from squamous metaplasia due to vitamin A deficiency.
eye.cond: Vitamin A deficiency (xerophthalmia), fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption (coeliac disease, IBD, chronic liver disease, cystic fibrosis)
eye.inv: Serum retinol (vitamin A), fat-soluble vitamin panel (A, D, E, K), anti-tTG (coeliac), LFT

eye.sig: Reduced tear film. Primary Sjogren's or secondary to autoimmune conditions, omega-3 deficiency, or environmental factors. Indicates anti-inflammatory fatty acid depletion.
eye.cond: Sjogren's syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis, SLE, omega-3 fatty acid deficiency, meibomian gland dysfunction, anticholinergic drug effects
eye.inv: Schirmer's test, ANA, anti-Ro (SSA) / anti-La (SSB) antibodies, omega-3 index, rheumatoid factor, ophthalmology assessment

skin.sig: Velvety, hyperpigmented thickening — axillae, neck, groin, under breasts. Driven by hyperinsulinaemia stimulating keratinocyte proliferation via IGF-1 receptors. Strong marker of insulin resistance.
skin.cond: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, insulin resistance, PCOS, obesity, acromegaly, Cushing's syndrome, rarely gastric adenocarcinoma (paraneoplastic)
skin.inv: Fasting insulin, HOMA-IR, fasting glucose, HbA1c, LH/FSH/DHEA-S (if PCOS suspected), IGF-1

skin.sig: Whealing in the path of firm stroking — mast cell hyperreactivity and histamine excess. Indicates gut-immune axis dysregulation and often correlates with intestinal hyperpermeability.
skin.cond: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS), chronic urticaria, IgE-mediated allergy, leaky gut syndrome, chronic histamine intolerance
skin.inv: Serum tryptase (elevated in MCAS), total IgE, 24h urine histamine, intestinal permeability markers (lactulose and mannitol), DAO enzyme activity

skin.sig: Autoimmune melanocyte destruction. Segmental (dermatomal) vs generalised patterns have different prognosis. Indicates systemic autoimmune diathesis — thyroid-gut-immune axis.
skin.cond: Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Graves' disease, type 1 diabetes mellitus, Addison's disease, alopecia areata, pernicious anaemia
skin.inv: TSH, anti-TPO, anti-thyroglobulin, fasting glucose, 8 AM serum cortisol, CBC, vitamin B12

skin.sig: Orange-yellow pigmentation of palms, soles, and nasolabial folds. Sclera remains white — this distinguishes it from jaundice. Caused by excess beta-carotene or impaired hepatic conversion (hypothyroidism).
skin.cond: Dietary excess (carrot, sweet potato, pumpkin), hypothyroidism (impairs beta-carotene to retinol conversion), renal failure, diabetes mellitus
skin.inv: TSH (screen for hypothyroid as conversion defect), serum carotene, LFT, fasting glucose. Key: sclera NOT yellow

skin.sig: Photosensitive dermatitis (Casal's necklace pattern — sun-exposed areas), diarrhoea, dementia, and death. Niacin (vitamin B3) deficiency. Also seen with tryptophan depletion.
skin.cond: Niacin (B3) deficiency, alcoholism, carcinoid syndrome (tryptophan diversion), isoniazid therapy (pyridoxine antagonism), malabsorption
skin.inv: Urine N-methylnicotinamide (diagnostic), serum niacin, plasma tryptophan, 24h urine 2-pyridone, B6, CBC

skin.sig: Fixed erythema over malar eminences bridging the nose — spares nasolabial folds. Hallmark of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. Indicates high autoimmune inflammatory burden and gut-immune axis disruption.
skin.cond: SLE (systemic lupus erythematosus), rosacea (mimicker — does not spare nasolabial folds), seborrhoeic dermatitis (mimicker)
skin.inv: ANA (screening), anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA, specific for SLE), complement C3 and C4, CBC with differential, urinalysis (protein, casts), CRP

skin.sig: Purplish or red wide striae suggest Cushing's syndrome or prolonged corticosteroid use. Silvery-white striae indicate physiological stretching. Abdominal and proximal limb striae are classic for hypercortisolism.
skin.cond: Cushing's syndrome, prolonged systemic corticosteroid therapy, rapid weight gain, puberty (physiological), pregnancy
skin.inv: 24h urine free cortisol, overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (1 mg), DHEA-S, morning ACTH, cortisol diurnal curve

skin.sig: Assessment of extracellular fluid excess. Pit depth and refill time determine grade. In naturopathic assessment, indicates lymphatic stagnation, renal or hepatic dysfunction, or hypoalbuminaemia.
skin.cond: Grade 1 (+1): 2 mm pit, immediate rebound — mild (venous insufficiency). Grade 2 (+2): 4 mm, 15-second rebound — moderate. Grade 3 (+3): 6 mm, 30-second rebound — significant (heart failure, nephrotic). Grade 4 (+4): 8 mm, >2-minute rebound — severe (anasarca)
skin.inv: Serum albumin, BNP (heart failure), eGFR (renal), LFT (hepatic), urinalysis (protein), echocardiography, TSH (hypothyroid oedema)

iridology.sig: Immediate ring around the pupil corresponds to the stomach in iridology. Discolouration or irregular contour interpreted as representing digestive strength and gastric secretion function.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: stomach, oesophagus, digestive enzyme activity
iridology.inv: Educational reference only — not validated diagnostically. Suggested: H. pylori test, SIBO breath test, gastric pH studies

iridology.sig: The autonomic nerve wreath (collarette) separates pupillary from ciliary zones. Irregular shape interpreted as reflecting intestinal function, absorption, and gut flora ecology.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: small intestine, large intestine, gut microbiome, lymphoid tissue (Peyer's patches)
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: SIBO breath test, stool PCR/culture, intestinal permeability markers, secretory IgA

iridology.sig: Upper temporal iris — right iris maps to liver and gallbladder; left iris maps to spleen and pancreas. Discolouration interpreted as hepatobiliary stress or blood sugar regulation.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: Right iris — liver, gallbladder, bile ducts. Left iris — spleen, pancreas
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: LFT, GGT, fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipase, amylase, USS abdomen

iridology.sig: Lateral mid-iris (3 o'clock right iris, 9 o'clock left iris) corresponds to kidneys and adrenal glands. Interpreted as reflecting stress response capacity, fluid balance, and renal filtration.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: kidneys, adrenal cortex, adrenal medulla, ureters
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: eGFR, urea, creatinine, 8 AM cortisol, DHEA-S, aldosterone-renin ratio, urinalysis

iridology.sig: Upper-nasal iris sector interpreted as representing bronchopulmonary function and lymphatic drainage. White or cloudy appearance interpreted as inflammatory or mucus-forming tendency.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: bronchi, lung parenchyma, upper respiratory passages, pleura
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: spirometry (FEV1 and FVC), peak flow, chest X-ray, SpO2, eosinophil count

iridology.sig: Lower iris (6 o'clock region, bilateral) interpreted as representing pelvic organs. Pigment spots or lacunae here draw attention to gynaecological or urological history.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: uterus, ovaries, fallopian tubes, prostate, bladder, rectum
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: pelvic USS, FSH/LH/oestradiol/progesterone, PSA, urinalysis, pap smear

iridology.sig: Outer ciliary zone interpreted as skin, lymphatics, thyroid, and peripheral circulation. Radially arranged white flecks (lymphatic rosary) interpreted as lymphatic congestion or inflammatory tendency.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: lymph nodes, thyroid gland, parathyroid, skin, peripheral lymphatics
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: TSH, anti-TPO, lymphocyte count, serum protein electrophoresis, LDH

iridology.sig: Oval or round openings in iris stroma — interpreted as indicating functional weakness in the corresponding organ zone. Size and depth interpreted as reflecting severity.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: interpreted as organ or tissue weakness, genetic predisposition, or prior trauma in corresponding body zone
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Correlate with clinical history. Validated investigation depends on zone.

iridology.sig: Wide, regular collarette = balanced autonomic tone. Contracted (large pupillary zone) = sympathetic dominance and stress response. Distorted = interpreted as functional gut-nervous system dysregulation.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: sympathetic-parasympathetic balance, HPA axis, gut-brain axis, vagal tone
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: HRV (heart rate variability), cortisol diurnal curve, DHEA-S, 24h urinary catecholamines

iridology.sig: White or grey outer ring on iris periphery — interpreted in iridology as sodium retention, mineralocorticoid excess, and cardiovascular stress. Overlaps with arcus senilis in conventional medicine.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: cardiovascular risk, sodium-water retention, adrenal stress
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: BP measurement, lipid profile, BUN, sodium and potassium, aldosterone-renin ratio

iridology.sig: Superior iris (12 o'clock position) interpreted as representing cerebral function, memory, cognitive capacity, and autonomic nervous system balance.
iridology.cond: Educational reference: cerebral cortex, cerebellum, brainstem, cranial nerves, pituitary gland
iridology.inv: Educational reference only. Suggested: B12, folate, diurnal cortisol, magnesium, omega-3 index, HRV assessment

iridology.sig: Iridology has NOT been validated as a diagnostic tool in randomised controlled trials. Multiple systematic reviews (Ernst, 2000; NCCIH) show no reliable correlation between iris features and organ pathology. Presented here as historical and educational reference within naturopathic tradition only.
iridology.cond: Iridology should NEVER substitute for clinical examination, validated diagnostics, or evidence-based investigations. Use as a dialogue tool and cultural context only.
iridology.inv: Standard evidence-based investigations (per clinical presentation) always take precedence. See other tabs for validated physical diagnosis signs.
